sections.heading: Teenage pregnancy fell overall, but not every measure improved
sections.body: Across the Philippines, 4.9 percent of teens had experienced pregnancy in 2025, down from 5.4 percent in 2022. Live birth also fell slightly, from 3.8 percent to 3.5 percent, and current pregnancy dropped from 1.6 percent to 1.3 percent. Pregnancy loss moved the other way, rising from 0.4 percent to 0.5 percent, so the overall decline does not mean every related measure went down.

sections.heading: Several regions were far above the national rate
sections.body: Zamboanga Peninsula¹ had the highest share of teens who experienced pregnancy in 2025, at 9.3 percent. That was almost double the national figure of 4.9 percent. MIMAROPA and SOCCSKSARGEN came next at 8.2 percent each, and both were also high for begun childbearing. This means the regional pattern is not about just one unusual area; several regions were far above the national level.

sections.heading: Some regions increased sharply even as the national rate fell
sections.body: SOCCSKSARGEN rose from 3.8 percent of teens who experienced pregnancy in 2022 to 8.2 percent in 2025. MIMAROPA also increased substantially, from 5.0 percent to 8.2 percent. These increases matter because the national rate declined during the same period, meaning the national trend hides sharp regional changes moving in different directions.

sections.heading: Some high-rate regions improved but still stayed above the national level
sections.body: Northern Mindanao fell from 10.9 percent in 2022 to 6.4 percent in 2025, and Caraga fell from 7.7 percent to 6.5 percent. Even with those declines, both were still above the national figure in 2025. The regional story is therefore a mix of areas that remain high and areas that changed recently, not just a simple ranking of current rates.

sections.heading: Current pregnancy points to a different regional pattern
sections.body: Negros Island Region had the highest current pregnancy measure at 3.8 percent, followed by Ilocos Region at 3.5 percent and MIMAROPA at 3.1 percent. These are not exactly the same regions that top the overall experienced-pregnancy ranking. This difference suggests that current pregnancy gives useful timing information beyond the cumulative measures of live birth and begun childbearing.

sections.heading: Pregnancy experience rises most at the oldest teen ages
sections.body: In 2025, the share of teens who experienced pregnancy rose from 1.5 percent at age 15 to 9.7 percent at age 19. The biggest one-year age jump in the 2025 series was from age 18 to 19, when the rate increased by 2.8 points. Age 19 also fell substantially from 13.3 percent in 2022, so the oldest teen group still had the highest rate but was below its 2022 level.

sections.heading: Rural teens had a higher rate, but regional gaps were larger
sections.body: Rural teens had a higher experienced-pregnancy rate than urban teens in 2025: 5.8 percent compared with 4.2 percent. Both groups declined from 2022, with rural teens down from 6.1 percent and urban teens down from 4.8 percent. The 1.6-point gap by residence is meaningful, but it is much smaller than the difference between the highest and lowest regions.

sections.heading: Rates were highest among teens with Grades 1-6 education
sections.body: Teens with Grades 1-6 education had the highest experienced-pregnancy rate in 2025, at 17.2 percent. Begun childbearing was also high for this group, at 16.2 percent. Grades 7-10 and Grades 11-12 were much lower at 4.7 percent and 5.1 percent, while college was lower still at 2.6 percent. The no-education and post-secondary rows have very small denominators or suppressed values, so the clearest comparison is between Grades 1-6 and the larger education groups.

sections.heading: Teenage pregnancy was much higher in the lowest wealth quintile
sections.body: The lowest wealth quintile had the highest experienced-pregnancy rate in 2025, at 9.4 percent, compared with 1.4 percent in the highest quintile. From 2022, the lowest, middle, and highest quintiles declined, while the second and fourth quintiles increased slightly. Pregnancy loss was also highest in the lowest quintile at 1.9 percent, adding another layer to the wealth pattern.

takeaways: Nationally, teenage pregnancy declined from 5.4 percent in 2022 to 4.9 percent in 2025.
Regional differences are wide, with Zamboanga Peninsula¹, MIMAROPA, and SOCCSKSARGEN far above the national level.
The places with the highest current-pregnancy levels are not exactly the same as the places with the highest cumulative teenage pregnancy levels.
Teenage pregnancy remains much higher among teens in the lowest wealth quintile and among teens with Grades 1-6 education.
footnotes: ¹ Includes the province of Sulu in 2025.
